case_name: Treatment of well water containing alachlor oxanilic acid (OA) for drinking water supply
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for well water contaminated with Alachlor Degradates intended for drinking water use under minimal chemical use constraints.

input_data.scenario_background: A rural community water system draws from a deep well and has detected alachlor oxanilic acid (OA), an alachlor degradate, in the raw water. The well water is otherwise relatively clean and stable, and the utility wants a proven treatment approach that minimizes ongoing chemical addition. The plant can handle routine filtration equipment and periodic media replacement, but it does not want a chemically intensive advanced oxidation program.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should achieve reliable reduction of alachlor oxanilic acid (OA) to support drinking water compliance and consumer protection.
secondary_goals: use established drinking water treatment practice

core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_adsorption: adsorb dissolved alachlor degradate organics from otherwise low-turbidity well water
core_unit_functions.post_filtration: retain carbon fines and provide polished finished water
core_unit_functions.disinfection: ensure microbiological safety in distributed drinking water

applicability_rationale: alachlor oxanilic acid (OA) is an organic micropollutant for which activated carbon is a conventional drinking water control option
the well water has low turbidity and low DOC, which supports effective carbon adsorption and longer media life
this approach uses little to no continuous chemical addition and aligns directly with the minimal chemical use constraint
operation is straightforward for a small utility with routine media monitoring and replacement capability
potential_risks: breakthrough can occur if carbon is not changed or regenerated on schedule
natural organic matter changes could shorten bed life
empty bed contact time must be adequate for reliable micropollutant removal

core_unit_functions.powdered_or_granular_activated_carbon_co: provide adsorption capacity for dissolved alachlor degradates with flexible carbon dosing or contact configuration
core_unit_functions.filtration: separate carbon particles or polish effluent after contact

applicability_rationale: activated carbon remains a well-supported approach for trace organic contaminant control in drinking water
a contact-and-filter configuration can be adapted where the utility wants carbon treatment without highly specialized systems
it still uses much less oxidant chemistry than advanced oxidation approaches
potential_risks: powdered carbon handling can add solids management complexity
performance depends on contact time and carbon dose selection
less preferred than fixed-bed GAC for consistent small-system operation

core_unit_functions.nanofiltration_or_reverse_osmosis: reject dissolved organic micropollutants including pesticide degradates
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust corrosivity and finished water mineral balance after membrane treatment

applicability_rationale: membranes can technically achieve strong removal of dissolved low-level organic contaminants
this option may be considered if future source water issues expand beyond a single degradate
the raw water quality is otherwise favorable for membrane pretreatment requirements
potential_risks: higher energy and operator attention than necessary for a single-contaminant case

core_unit_functions.uv_hydrogen_peroxide_advanced_oxidation: oxidize dissolved organic contaminants through hydroxyl radical reactions
core_unit_functions.biological_or_gac_polishing: remove oxidation byproducts and provide final organic polishing

applicability_rationale: advanced oxidation can degrade certain organic micropollutants when adsorption alone is insufficient
a polishing step is commonly paired to capture transformation products
it is technically plausible but not naturally aligned with the stated desire for minimal chemical use
potential_risks: requires continuous oxidant chemical addition and UV system control
may form intermediate byproducts that still need downstream removal
greater operational complexity than needed for this relatively clean well water

core_unit_functions.air_stripping: remove volatile compounds by phase transfer to air

applicability_rationale: included only as a low-priority contrast option from established contaminant-control practice
it is generally used for volatile organics rather than polar pesticide degradates like alachlor oxanilic acid
potential_risks: likely ineffective for alachlor oxanilic acid because the compound is not suitably volatile
could misallocate capital without meaningful contaminant reduction

must_reflect: minimal chemical use should favor adsorption over oxidation-heavy treatment
simple robust operation should favor fixed-bed carbon systems
membrane and advanced oxidation options should not outrank conventional carbon adsorption in this case

must_contain: why activated carbon is appropriate for organic micropollutants such as alachlor oxanilic acid
why low turbidity and low DOC well water support adsorption treatment
why advanced oxidation ranks lower because it conflicts with minimal chemical use
why membrane options rank lower because of concentrate and complexity burden

should_mention: carbon breakthrough and media replacement needs
competition from natural organic matter even if low
possible transformation byproducts from advanced oxidation
